source_paper_title: Can Learning Be Explained by Local Optimality in Robust Low-Rank Matrix Recovery?
context:
Section 2.3 (Asymmetric matrix completion), discussion immediately after Theorem 6, page 12.

We note that this result does not refute the possibility of existing highly rank-imbalanced solutions—in particular, those with \(\mathrm{rank}(W_1^\star)+\mathrm{rank}(W_2^\star)\ge k\)—as local optima. Although our result does not refute this possibility, we conjecture that highly rank-imbalanced solutions are unlikely to emerge as local optima. We leave a rigorous proof of this conjecture for future work.
title: Prove highly rank-imbalanced true solutions are not local optima in noisy asymmetric matrix completion with \(\ell_1\)-loss
background_latex:
Let \(d_1,d_2\in\mathbb{N}\), \(r\le \min\{d_1,d_2\}\), and let the ground-truth matrix be \(X^\star\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times d_2}\) with \(\mathrm{rank}(X^\star)=r\). Fix a search rank \(k\ge r\) and factor variables \(W_1\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times k}\), \(W_2\in\mathbb{R}^{k\times d_2}\), and write \(W=(W_1,W_2)\).

(Matrix completion model) Let \(\Omega\subseteq[d_1]\times[d_2]\) be a random set where each index \((\alpha,\beta)\) is included independently with probability \(s\in[0,1]\). Let \(m=|\Omega|\). Define the observed matrix \(Y\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times d_2}\) by \(Y[\alpha,\beta]=X^\star[\alpha,\beta]+E[\alpha,\beta]\) for \((\alpha,\beta)\in\Omega\), and \(Y[\alpha,\beta]=0\) otherwise.

(Noise model) For each observed entry \((\alpha,\beta)\in\Omega\), the noise \(E[\alpha,\beta]\) is independently nonzero with probability \(p\in[0,1]\); conditional on being nonzero it is drawn from a distribution \(P_o\). A random variable \(\zeta\sim P_o\) satisfies \(\mathbb{P}(|\zeta|\ge t_0)\ge p_0\) for constants \(t_0>0\) and \(p_0\in(0,1]\).

Define the (asymmetric) Burer--Monteiro \(\ell_1\) matrix-completion objective
\[f^{c}_{\ell_1}(W_1,W_2):=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{(\alpha,\beta)\in\Omega}\bigl|Y[\alpha,\beta]-(W_1W_2)[\alpha,\beta]\bigr|.\]

Define the set of true solutions
\[\mathcal{W}:=\{(W_1,W_2): W_1W_2=X^\star\}.\]
For \(s\in\mathbb{N}\), define \(\mathcal{W}(s):=\{(W_1,W_2)\in\mathcal{W}: \mathrm{rank}(W_1)+\mathrm{rank}(W_2)\le s\}\). Call a true solution \((W_1,W_2)\in\mathcal{W}\) highly rank-imbalanced if \(\mathrm{rank}(W_1)+\mathrm{rank}(W_2)\ge k\).

A point \(W^\star\) is a local optimum of \(f^{c}_{\ell_1}\) if there exists \(\varepsilon>0\) such that \(f^{c}_{\ell_1}(W)\ge f^{c}_{\ell_1}(W^\star)\) for all \(W\) with \(\|W-W^\star\|_F\le \varepsilon\).
problem_definition_latex: Prove (under explicit conditions on \(d_1,d_2,r,k,s,p,t_0,p_0\) consistent with Assumptions 2--3 in the paper) that with high probability over \(\Omega\) and the noise \(E\), no highly rank-imbalanced true solution \((W_1,W_2)\in\mathcal{W}\) is a local optimum of \(f^{c}_{\ell_1}\); i.e., show that for every \((W_1,W_2)\in\mathcal{W}\) with \(\mathrm{rank}(W_1)+\mathrm{rank}(W_2)\ge k\), there exists a perturbation \((\Delta W_1,\Delta W_2)\) and \(\gamma>0\) such that \(f^{c}_{\ell_1}(W_1+\gamma\Delta W_1,\,W_2+\gamma\Delta W_2)< f^{c}_{\ell_1}(W_1,W_2)\).
latex:
Let \(d_1,d_2\in\mathbb{N}\), \(r\le \min\{d_1,d_2\}\), and let the ground-truth matrix be \(X^\star\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times d_2}\) with \(\mathrm{rank}(X^\star)=r\). Fix a search rank \(k\ge r\) and factor variables \(W_1\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times k}\), \(W_2\in\mathbb{R}^{k\times d_2}\), and write \(W=(W_1,W_2)\).

(Matrix completion model) Let \(\Omega\subseteq[d_1]\times[d_2]\) be a random set where each index \((\alpha,\beta)\) is included independently with probability \(s\in[0,1]\). Let \(m=|\Omega|\). Define the observed matrix \(Y\in\mathbb{R}^{d_1\times d_2}\) by \(Y[\alpha,\beta]=X^\star[\alpha,\beta]+E[\alpha,\beta]\) for \((\alpha,\beta)\in\Omega\), and \(Y[\alpha,\beta]=0\) otherwise.

(Noise model) For each observed entry \((\alpha,\beta)\in\Omega\), the noise \(E[\alpha,\beta]\) is independently nonzero with probability \(p\in[0,1]\); conditional on being nonzero it is drawn from a distribution \(P_o\). A random variable \(\zeta\sim P_o\) satisfies \(\mathbb{P}(|\zeta|\ge t_0)\ge p_0\) for constants \(t_0>0\) and \(p_0\in(0,1]\).

Define the (asymmetric) Burer--Monteiro \(\ell_1\) matrix-completion objective
\[f^{c}_{\ell_1}(W_1,W_2):=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{(\alpha,\beta)\in\Omega}\bigl|Y[\alpha,\beta]-(W_1W_2)[\alpha,\beta]\bigr|.\]

Define the set of true solutions
\[\mathcal{W}:=\{(W_1,W_2): W_1W_2=X^\star\}.\]
For \(s\in\mathbb{N}\), define \(\mathcal{W}(s):=\{(W_1,W_2)\in\mathcal{W}: \mathrm{rank}(W_1)+\mathrm{rank}(W_2)\le s\}\). Call a true solution \((W_1,W_2)\in\mathcal{W}\) highly rank-imbalanced if \(\mathrm{rank}(W_1)+\mathrm{rank}(W_2)\ge k\).

A point \(W^\star\) is a local optimum of \(f^{c}_{\ell_1}\) if there exists \(\varepsilon>0\) such that \(f^{c}_{\ell_1}(W)\ge f^{c}_{\ell_1}(W^\star)\) for all \(W\) with \(\|W-W^\star\|_F\le \varepsilon\).

Prove (under explicit conditions on \(d_1,d_2,r,k,s,p,t_0,p_0\) consistent with Assumptions 2--3 in the paper) that with high probability over \(\Omega\) and the noise \(E\), no highly rank-imbalanced true solution \((W_1,W_2)\in\mathcal{W}\) is a local optimum of \(f^{c}_{\ell_1}\); i.e., show that for every \((W_1,W_2)\in\mathcal{W}\) with \(\mathrm{rank}(W_1)+\mathrm{rank}(W_2)\ge k\), there exists a perturbation \((\Delta W_1,\Delta W_2)\) and \(\gamma>0\) such that \(f^{c}_{\ell_1}(W_1+\gamma\Delta W_1,\,W_2+\gamma\Delta W_2)< f^{c}_{\ell_1}(W_1,W_2)\).
